mitre_attack: T1069.003 - Permission Groups Discovery: Cloud Groups
cwe: CWE-732 - Incorrect Permission Assignment for Critical Resource

threat.title: IAM Over-Privileged Roles in AWS Lambda and EC2 Leading to Privilege Escalation
threat.description: AWS Lambda functions and EC2 instances configured with overly permissive IAM roles (such as AdministratorAccess or broad wildcard permissions) allow attackers who compromise the compute resource to escalate privileges and access sensitive resources across the AWS account. This is one of the most critical cloud security misconfigurations, enabling lateral movement, data exfiltration, and full account takeover.

attack.scenario:
1. Attacker exploits a vulnerability in a Lambda function (e.g., command injection, SSRF, or dependency vulnerability)
2. Gains code execution within the Lambda runtime environment
3. Discovers Lambda's execution role has overly broad permissions (e.g., iam:*, s3:*, ec2:*, lambda:*)
4. Uses AWS SDK or CLI to enumerate IAM policies and discover excessive privileges
5. Escalates privileges by creating new IAM users, assuming admin roles, or accessing sensitive S3 buckets
6. Exfiltrates data from S3, RDS snapshots, or Secrets Manager; deploys crypto-miners on EC2; or establishes persistence through backdoor IAM users
prerequisites: Lambda function or EC2 instance with attached IAM role
IAM role has excessive permissions (wildcards, full access policies)
Attacker has initial code execution capability (RCE, SSRF, compromised credentials)
No IAM permission boundaries or SCPs limiting privilege escalation
Lack of runtime security monitoring or anomaly detection

cve_references.description: Container escape vulnerability in runC affecting AWS ECS and Lambda allowing privilege escalation to host
Laravel debug mode RCE exploited to access AWS metadata service and steal IAM credentials
SSRF in Spring Cloud Gateway allowing access to AWS metadata endpoint and IAM role credentials

real_world_breaches.impact: 106 million customers affected; $80 million fine from OCC; $190 million class-action settlement
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Over-privileged EC2 IAM role allowed SSRF exploitation via WAF misconfiguration, enabling attacker to list and download S3 buckets containing customer data

real_world_breaches.impact: Email addresses, hashed passwords, and API keys of customers leaked through AWS S3 misconfiguration
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Overly permissive IAM roles on AWS resources allowed unauthorized data access

bug_bounty_reports.summary: Lambda function with admin IAM role accessible via API Gateway allowing full AWS account compromise

security_research.title: AWS IAM Privilege Escalation Techniques
security_research.key_finding: Identified 21 different IAM privilege escalation methods in AWS, many relying on over-privileged roles

security_research.title: CIS Amazon Web Services Foundations Benchmark v2.0
security_research.key_finding: IAM principle of least privilege is the #1 recommended control for AWS security

security_research.title: Cloud Security Alliance - IAM Configuration Security in Cloud Computing
security_research.key_finding: 67% of cloud breaches involve misconfigured IAM permissions or overly permissive roles

mitigations.title: Implement AWS IAM Least Privilege Policies with Service-Specific Permissions
mitigations.description: Replace wildcard (*) permissions and AWS managed AdministratorAccess policies with fine-grained, service-specific IAM policies that grant only the minimum permissions required for Lambda and EC2 to perform their intended functions.
iso27001_controls: A.9.1.2 - Access to networks and network services
A.9.2.3 - Management of privileged access rights
nist_csf: PR.PT-3 - Least privilege and separation of duties principles are implemented
validation_steps: Review all Lambda execution roles and EC2 instance profiles for wildcard permissions
Use AWS Access Analyzer to identify unused permissions
Test Lambda/EC2 functionality with restricted policies in staging
Remove AdministratorAccess and PowerUserAccess managed policies
Implement IAM Permission Boundaries for additional guardrails

mitigations.title: Enable AWS IAM Access Analyzer for Continuous Permission Monitoring
mitigations.description: Deploy IAM Access Analyzer to continuously analyze IAM policies, detect overly permissive roles, and identify unused permissions. This provides automated detection of privilege escalation risks and external resource access.

code_example.code:
# AWS CDK (Python) - Deploy IAM Access Analyzer
from aws_cdk import (
    Stack,
    aws_accessanalyzer as accessanalyzer,
    aws_iam as iam,
    aws_sns as sns,
    aws_cloudwatch as cloudwatch,
    aws_cloudwatch_actions as cw_actions
)
from constructs import Construct

class IAMAccessAnalyzerStack(Stack):
    def __init__(self, scope: Construct, id: str, **kwargs):
        super().__init__(scope, id, **kwargs)
        
        # Create SNS topic for alerts
        alert_topic = sns.Topic(
            self, "IAMAccessAnalyzerAlerts",
            display_name="IAM Access Analyzer Findings"
        )
        
        # Create IAM Access Analyzer for organization
        analyzer = accessanalyzer.CfnAnalyzer(
            self, "OrganizationAnalyzer",
            type="ORGANIZATION",  # or "ACCOUNT" for single account
            analyzer_name="iam-least-privilege-analyzer",
            tags=[
                {"key": "Environment", "value": "Production"},
                {"key": "Purpose", "value": "Security"},
                {"key": "Compliance", "value": "CIS-AWS-Benchmark"}
            ]
        )
        
        # Create EventBridge rule for high-severity findings
        from aws_cdk import aws_events as events
        from aws_cdk import aws_events_targets as targets
        
        finding_rule = events.Rule(
            self, "AccessAnalyzerFindingRule",
            event_pattern=events.EventPattern(
                source=["aws.access-analyzer"],
                detail_type=["Access Analyzer Finding"],
                detail={
                    "status": ["ACTIVE"],
                    "resourceType": ["AWS::IAM::Role", "AWS::Lambda::Function"]
                }
            )
        )
        finding_rule.add_target(targets.SnsTopic(alert_topic))
        
        # CloudWatch alarm for critical findings
        metric = cloudwatch.Metric(
            namespace="AWS/AccessAnalyzer",
            metric_name="ActiveFindings",
            dimensions_map={"AnalyzerName": analyzer.analyzer_name}
        )
        
        alarm = cloudwatch.Alarm(
            self, "CriticalIAMFindingsAlarm",
            metric=metric,
            threshold=1,
            evaluation_periods=1,
            comparison_operator=cloudwatch.ComparisonOperator.GREATER_THAN_OR_EQUAL_TO_THRESHOLD,
            alarm_description="Alert on new IAM Access Analyzer findings"
        )
        alarm.add_alarm_action(cw_actions.SnsAction(alert_topic))

# Terraform equivalent
# resource "aws_accessanalyzer_analyzer" "main" {
#   analyzer_name = "iam-least-privilege-analyzer"
#   type          = "ORGANIZATION"
#   tags = {
#     Environment = "Production"
#     Purpose     = "Security"
#   }
# }

nist_csf: DE.CM-1 - Networks and systems are monitored
DE.CM-7 - Monitoring for unauthorized activities is performed
validation_steps: Deploy IAM Access Analyzer in organization or account scope
Configure SNS notifications for new findings
Review initial findings and create remediation plan
Integrate findings into Security Hub or SIEM
Set up automated policy generation based on CloudTrail logs

mitigations.title: Enforce Service-Specific IAM Roles with Permission Boundaries and SCPs
mitigations.description: Implement IAM Permission Boundaries and AWS Service Control Policies (SCPs) to create defense-in-depth guardrails that prevent privilege escalation even if IAM policies are misconfigured. Use separate roles per Lambda function and EC2 instance.
iso27001_controls: A.9.2.3 - Management of privileged access rights
A.9.4.4 - Use of privileged utility programs
nist_csf: PR.AC-4 - Access permissions and authorizations are managed
PR.PT-3 - Least privilege principles are incorporated
validation_steps: Define permission boundaries for all compute roles
Create separate IAM roles per Lambda function
Test that roles cannot escalate privileges
Implement SCPs at AWS Organizations level
Monitor IAM changes via CloudTrail and Config

indicators: IAM role assumption from unexpected IP addresses or regions
Unusual API calls to IAM services (CreateUser, AttachRolePolicy, AssumeRole)
Lambda or EC2 accessing S3 buckets outside normal scope
Elevated privilege API calls during off-hours
Multiple AssumeRole calls in short time period
Creation of new IAM users or access keys from compute resources
Access to Secrets Manager or Parameter Store secrets not normally used
Unusual data transfer volumes from S3 or RDS

log_queries.query:
fields @timestamp, userIdentity.principalId, eventName, sourceIPAddress, errorCode
| filter eventSource = "iam.amazonaws.com"
| filter eventName in ["CreateUser", "CreateAccessKey", "AttachUserPolicy", "AttachRolePolicy", "PutUserPolicy", "PutRolePolicy", "AssumeRole"]
| filter userIdentity.type = "AssumedRole"
| filter userIdentity.principalId like /i-[a-z0-9]+/ or userIdentity.principalId like /AROA[A-Z0-9]+/
| stats count(*) as api_calls by userIdentity.principalId, eventName, sourceIPAddress
| sort api_calls desc

index=aws sourcetype=aws:cloudtrail eventSource=iam.amazonaws.com
(eventName=CreateUser OR eventName=CreateAccessKey OR eventName=AttachUserPolicy OR eventName=AttachRolePolicy OR eventName=PutUserPolicy OR eventName=AssumeRole)
userIdentity.type=AssumedRole
| eval role_name=mvindex(split(userIdentity.principalId, ":"), 1)
| stats count by eventName, role_name, sourceIPAddress, userAgent
| where count > 5
| sort -count

event.dataset: "aws.cloudtrail" AND 
event.provider: "iam.amazonaws.com" AND
event.action: ("CreateUser" OR "CreateAccessKey" OR "AttachUserPolicy" OR "AttachRolePolicy" OR "AssumeRole") AND
cloud.account.id: "123456789012" AND
user.type: "AssumedRole"
| stats count by event.action, user.name, source.ip

tools.name: AWS GuardDuty
tools.usage: Automatically detects IAM credential exfiltration and privilege escalation attempts
